name: Brute Force: Store Values
summary: Collect all node values into an array, then read them back in reverse into a result list — reverses values without touching pointers, at the cost of O(n) extra space.
visualizationIntent: check: empty list guard highlighted. init: head_links built (sequential arrows shown on the linked list), values array shown empty, pos at index 0. loop-collect: current node highlighted via pos pointer, value appended to values aux array which grows. move-collect: pos advances to next node. loop-reverse: i pointer on values aux array moves from back to front; each value appended to result. return: result shown as the reversed sequence.

lineExplanations.3: Edge-case guard: if head is empty, there is nothing to reverse.
lineExplanations.4: Initialise result as an empty list to return for the empty-input case.
lineExplanations.5: Return immediately — no reversal needed for an empty list.
lineExplanations.6: Build head_links: the sequential next-pointer array for the linked list — node i points to i+1; the last node points to -1 (null).
lineExplanations.7: Create an empty array called values that will store every node's value in forward order.
lineExplanations.8: Set pos to 0 — the index of the first node in head.
lineExplanations.9: First loop header: iterate while pos is within bounds of the list.
lineExplanations.10: Append head[pos] to values — collecting node values left-to-right.
lineExplanations.11: Advance pos by 1 — move to the next node.
lineExplanations.12: Initialise the result list that will hold the reversed values.
lineExplanations.13: Set i to the last valid index of values — we will read from right to left.
lineExplanations.14: Second loop header: iterate while i is a valid index (>= 0).
lineExplanations.15: Append values[i] to result — reading from back to front reverses the order.
lineExplanations.16: Decrement i — move one step left toward the front of values.
lineExplanations.17: Return the fully reversed result list.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the container class the Knacktor platform expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def reverseList(self, head): — head is a Python list of integers, one per node.
syntaxExplanations.3: if not head: — 'not head' is True when head is an empty list [].
syntaxExplanations.4: result = [] — creates an empty Python list using the [] literal.
syntaxExplanations.5: return result — exits the function early, skipping all reversal logic.
syntaxExplanations.6: head_links = list(range(1, len(head))) + [-1] — range(1, n) gives [1,2,...,n-1]; appending -1 makes the last node point to null.
syntaxExplanations.7: values = [] — an empty list that will grow as we collect node values.
syntaxExplanations.8: pos = 0 — an integer index; 0 means the first position (Python is 0-indexed).
syntaxExplanations.9: while pos < len(head): — len(head) gives the number of elements; loop runs n times.
syntaxExplanations.10: values.append(head[pos]) — .append() adds one element to the end of the list.
syntaxExplanations.11: pos = pos + 1 — increments the index; same as pos += 1.
syntaxExplanations.12: result = [] — a second fresh list; will hold our answer.
syntaxExplanations.13: i = len(values) - 1 — len(values) - 1 is the last valid index in a 0-indexed list.
syntaxExplanations.14: while i >= 0: — loop runs from index len-1 down to 0 (inclusive).
syntaxExplanations.15: result.append(values[i]) — reads the element at index i and adds it to result.
syntaxExplanations.16: i = i - 1 — decrements i; same as i -= 1.
syntaxExplanations.17: return result — hands the reversed list back to the caller.
